title: Septic shock: lactic acidosis from hypoperfusion
vignette: An 87-year-old nursing home resident is brought to the emergency department with a 2-day history of fever, vomiting, and progressive lethargy. The patient's past medical history is significant for advanced dementia and stroke. Temperature is 38.3 C (101 F), blood pressure is 88/62 mm Hg, and pulse is 120/min and regular. On examination, he is lethargic but arousable. Coarse rhonchi are heard over the right midlung. Laboratory results show leukocytes 23,000/mm3, sodium 140 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 18 mEq/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 121 mg/dL, and lactic acid 4.0 mmol/L (normal: <2 mmol/L). Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's increased anion gap?

clues.note: Systemic illness with altered mental status in a frail elder. Combined with leukocytosis and a lung source, this is sepsis — the setup for shock-related lactic acidosis.

clues.text: blood pressure is 88/62 mm Hg, and pulse is 120/min
clues.note: Hypotension + tachycardia = shock with tissue hypoperfusion. Inadequate oxygen delivery forces tissues into anaerobic metabolism, the engine of lactic acid production.

clues.text: Coarse rhonchi are heard over the right midlung
clues.note: Localizes the infectious source to pneumonia, the likely cause of septic shock here. Source identification supports the sepsis → hypoperfusion → lactate chain.

A high lactate (4.0 / normal <2) with low bicarbonate (18 / normal 22-28) is the unmeasured anion raising the gap. Lactate accumulates when hypoperfused cells can't run oxidative phosphorylation and shunt pyruvate to lactate.

whatMatters: The frailty/dementia details are window dressing. The core chain is sepsis (pneumonia) → hypotension/tachycardia → tissue hypoperfusion → the cells can't run oxidative phosphorylation → pyruvate is shunted to lactate → high-anion-gap lactic acidosis. The asked cause is DECREASED oxidative phosphorylation — answer A.
strategy: For an elevated anion gap, name the unmeasured anion (MUDPILES). Here lactate is explicitly high, so trace WHY: shock → low O2 delivery → mitochondria can't oxidatively phosphorylate → anaerobic glycolysis → lactate. Pick the upstream mechanism (impaired oxidative phosphorylation), not a downstream/clearance detail.

options.text: Reduced renal excretion of organic acids

eliminations.label: hepatic gluconeogenesis defect — inborn errors, wrong patient
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: primary impairment of hepatic gluconeogenesis (pyruvate carboxylase or glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency) can raise lactate. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: these inborn errors present in infancy with fasting hypoglycemia, not an 87-year-old with septic shock; his glucose is 121. ✅ Correct if: the patient were an infant with fasting hypoglycemia and lactic acidosis.

eliminations.label: ketoacidosis — wrong anion
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: beta-hydroxybutyrate/acetoacetate cause the gap in DKA and alcoholic/starvation ketoacidosis. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: he isn't diabetic-uncontrolled or starving (glucose 121), and the stem names LACTATE as the elevated anion, not ketones. ✅ Correct if: the stem gave high glucose with ketones, a fruity breath, or alcohol/starvation.

eliminations.label: renal acid retention — a uremic/RTA story, not this
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: reduced renal excretion of organic acids contributes to the gap in renal failure (uremia). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: creatinine is normal (0.9) and the acid here is lactate generated by hypoperfusion, not retained by failing kidneys. ✅ Correct if: creatinine/BUN were markedly elevated pointing to uremic acidosis.

eliminations.label: impaired hepatic lactate clearance — secondary, not the primary driver (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the liver normally clears lactate via gluconeogenesis; hypoperfusion can reduce this and worsen the level. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: in septic shock the dominant mechanism is OVERPRODUCTION from anaerobic metabolism (failed oxidative phosphorylation); reduced clearance is a contributing, downstream factor, not the primary cause being asked. ✅ Correct if: the stem isolated severe hepatic failure as the sole cause of lactate accumulation.

reasoning: Tissue hypoperfusion → inadequate O2 delivery to mitochondria
Cells can't run oxidative phosphorylation → pyruvate → lactate
High-anion-gap lactic acidosis from decreased oxidative phosphorylation → answer A
mechanism: In septic shock, end-organ hypoperfusion lowers oxygen delivery so mitochondria cannot complete oxidative phosphorylation. Glycolysis continues, generating pyruvate and NADH; without adequate O2, pyruvate is reduced to lactate (regenerating NAD+). Lactate is an unmeasured anion, so it raises the anion gap and consumes bicarbonate (here 18). Reduced hepatic lactate clearance contributes, but overproduction from anaerobic metabolism is the primary driver.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Physiology/Pathology > Acid-base: high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (MUDPILES — lactate is the 'L'), the causes-of-lactic-acidosis table (reduced O2 delivery in shock), and oxidative phosphorylation basics. Cross-link sepsis/shock physiology.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Reframe to a chain: shock → ↓O2 delivery → ↓oxidative phosphorylation → anaerobic glycolysis → lactate → ↑gap. The question wants the UPSTREAM mechanism, not clearance.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on anion gap, oxidative phosphorylation, anaerobic glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, MUDPILES? Tell me which term and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Option E (reduced hepatic lactate metabolism) is the tempting near-miss because the liver does clear lactate. In septic shock, OVERPRODUCTION dominates — choose decreased oxidative phosphorylation.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Answer choices B-E here were reconstructed because the screenshot cut them off (only the correct choice A was visible). The correct answer A and the teaching point are solid; verify the exact wording of B-E against UWorld if you want them word-perfect.

keywords.back: ↓ O2 delivery → ↓ oxidative phosphorylation → anaerobic glycolysis

keywords.front: Inborn errors causing infantile lactic acidosis

keywords.back: Reduced to lactate by LDH (regenerates NAD+)

firstAid.detail: Physiology > Acid-base: causes of lactic acidosis (reduced O2 delivery/shock, mitochondrial dysfunction), oxidative phosphorylation, anion gap calculation.
firstAid.location: Physiology > Acid-base: causes of lactic acidosis (reduced O2 delivery/shock, mitochondrial dysfunction), oxidative phosphorylation, anion gap calculation.

firstAid.detail: Path/Physio: MUDPILES — ketoacidosis (DKA/alcoholic), uremia, toxins; plus inborn errors of gluconeogenesis for infantile lactic acidosis.
firstAid.location: Path/Physio: MUDPILES — ketoacidosis (DKA/alcoholic), uremia, toxins; plus inborn errors of gluconeogenesis for infantile lactic acidosis.

firstAid.detail: Physiology/Path: SIRS/sepsis criteria, distributive shock hemodynamics, end-organ hypoperfusion and lactate as a marker.
firstAid.location: Physiology/Path: SIRS/sepsis criteria, distributive shock hemodynamics, end-organ hypoperfusion and lactate as a marker.

memoryHook: No O2 to the mitochondria → no oxidative phosphorylation → pyruvate becomes LACTATE → the gap opens. Shock makes lactate.
